title: Dart Null Safety Mastery Test
description: Master Dart's null safety features including nullable and non-nullable types, assertion operators, late initialization, and sound null safety principles

questions.text: What symbol is used to declare a nullable variable in Dart?
questions.explanation: The '?' symbol is appended to the type to declare a nullable variable. Example: `String? name` means name can hold a String or null.

questions.text: What happens when you try to assign null to a non-nullable variable?
questions.explanation: Dart's sound null safety catches this at compile time. Non-nullable variables cannot be assigned null, preventing null pointer exceptions at runtime.

questions.text: What does the '!' operator do in null safety?
options: Asserts that an expression is non-null
questions.explanation: The '!' operator (bang operator) tells Dart that you're certain an expression isn't null. It converts a nullable type to non-nullable, but throws if null at runtime.
hints: Use when you know value isn't null
Can cause runtime error if wrong

questions.text: What is the purpose of the 'late' keyword?
options: To declare a variable that can be null
To defer initialization of a non-nullable variable
questions.explanation: 'late' defers initialization of a non-nullable variable until it's first accessed. It's useful for dependency injection or when initialization depends on 'this'.
hints: Must be initialized before first use

questions.text: What is the null-aware coalescing operator '??'?
options: Returns left if null, otherwise right
Returns left if not null, otherwise right
Checks if both sides are null
questions.explanation: The '??' operator returns the left expression if it's NOT null, otherwise returns the right expression. Example: `name ?? 'Guest'`

questions.text: What does the null-aware assignment operator '??=' do?
options: Assigns value only if variable is not null
Assigns value only if variable is null
questions.explanation: '??=' assigns the right-hand value only if the left-hand variable is currently null. Example: `name ??= 'Guest'` sets name only if it was null.

questions.text: What is the null-aware access operator '?.'?
options: Accesses property only if object is not null
questions.explanation: '?.' safely accesses a property or method: if the object is null, the expression returns null instead of throwing an error. Example: `person?.name`
name is null, so the '??' operator returns the right expression 'Default'. The output is 'Default'.

questions.text: What happens if you use '!' on a null value?
questions.explanation: Using '!' on a null value throws a runtime exception (TypeError). The bang operator asserts the value isn't null, so null violates that assertion.
hints: Use only when certain value isn't null

questions.text: Which of these is a valid non-nullable variable declaration?
questions.explanation: Non-nullable variables cannot be assigned null. 'String name = 'John'' is valid. 'String name = null' would be a compile-time error.
hints: Must always have a non-null value

questions.text: What is type promotion in null safety?
options: Dart automatically treats nullable as non-nullable after null check
questions.explanation: After checking that a nullable variable isn't null, Dart 'promotes' it to non-nullable within that scope, allowing safe access without '!'.
hints: No need for '!' after if (x != null)

questions.text: What is the correct way to make a parameter required and nullable?
questions.explanation: The 'required' keyword comes first, then the type with '?' for nullable. Example: `required String? name` means name must be provided but can be null.
